title: Kubernetes in 2026 — A Beginner's Guide to Container Orchestration
excerpt: Kubernetes is mentioned in 60% of senior DevOps job descriptions. You do not need to understand every corner of it — you need to understand the core concepts that appear in every production deployment.

seo.metaTitle: Kubernetes for Beginners 2026 — Complete Guide to K8s | ArivuOn Academy
seo.metaDescription: Learn Kubernetes from scratch in 2026. Pods, deployments, services, ingress and real AWS EKS deployment explained for beginners — ArivuOn Academy.

content.text: Kubernetes runs Netflix, Spotify, Airbnb, and the majority of large-scale software infrastructure globally. It is the operating system of the cloud-native world. But its documentation is notoriously dense, and most tutorials assume you already understand half of it. This guide starts at zero.
Why Kubernetes Exists — The Problem It Solves

Docker lets you package applications into containers. But when you run 50 containers across 10 servers in production, who decides which container runs on which server? What happens when a container crashes? How do you update 50 containers with zero downtime? How do you scale from 50 to 5000 containers when traffic spikes? This is the problem Kubernetes solves. It is a container orchestration platform — the manager that decides where, when and how your containers run.
Core Kubernetes Concepts — The Mental Model

steps.title: Pod — The Smallest Unit
skills: One or more containers running together
steps.why: Everything in Kubernetes revolves around Pods. Understand that a Pod is temporary by design — Kubernetes replaces dead Pods automatically.

steps.title: Deployment — How You Run Applications
skills: Declares desired state (3 replicas of nginx)
Rollback to previous version in seconds
steps.why: Deployments are how you run every real application in Kubernetes. You say 'I want 3 replicas' and Kubernetes makes it happen and keeps it that way.

steps.title: Service — How Pods Talk to Each Other
skills: Stable DNS name for a set of Pods
Load balances across all matching Pods
steps.why: Pods have ephemeral IP addresses that change when they restart. Services provide a stable endpoint. This is the glue that makes microservices work.

steps.title: Ingress — HTTP Routing to Services
skills: Works with nginx-ingress or AWS ALB ingress
steps.why: Ingress is how traffic from the internet gets to your applications. One Ingress controller replaces multiple load balancers, saving significant cost.

steps.title: ConfigMap and Secret
skills: Injected as environment variables or files
steps.why: Never bake configuration into Docker images. ConfigMaps and Secrets let you deploy the same image to dev, staging, and production with different configuration.

content.title: A Production Deployment YAML — What You'll Write
content.code:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: arivuon-api
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: arivuon-api
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: arivuon-api
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: api
        image: your-registry/arivuon-api:v2.1.0
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
        env:
        - name: DATABASE_URL
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: app-secrets
              key: database-url
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "256Mi"
            cpu: "250m"
          limits:
            memory: "512Mi"
            cpu: "500m"
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 10

content.title: The Biggest Mistake When Learning Kubernetes
content.text: Trying to learn Kubernetes without first mastering Docker. Kubernetes orchestrates containers — if you do not understand how containers work, ports, volumes, and networking, Kubernetes will be incomprehensible. Spend 3-4 weeks with Docker first.
Frequently Asked Questions

items.question: Do I need to learn Kubernetes to be a DevOps engineer?
items.answer: For most DevOps roles in 2026 — yes. Kubernetes appears in 60%+ of DevOps job descriptions at companies with more than 50 engineers. However, you do not need to be a Kubernetes administrator. Understanding core concepts, deploying applications, debugging Pod issues, and working with Helm charts covers 90% of what most DevOps engineers need.

items.question: Kubernetes vs Docker Compose — which should I use?
items.answer: Docker Compose for local development and small applications (1-5 services, single machine). Kubernetes for production, multi-machine, multi-service deployments that need auto-scaling, self-healing, and zero-downtime deployments. The answer is usually: both — Docker Compose locally, Kubernetes in production.

items.question: What is the easiest way to start a Kubernetes cluster?
items.answer: For learning: use Minikube (single-node cluster on your laptop) or kind (Kubernetes in Docker). For production: use a managed service — AWS EKS, Google GKE, or Azure AKS. Managed services handle control plane management, upgrades, and high availability automatically. Never run your own Kubernetes control plane unless you have a dedicated platform team.

title: React Server Components in 2026 — What They Are and Why They Change Everything
excerpt: React Server Components fundamentally change how React applications are built. If you are still building SPAs the 2021 way, you are missing the most important React evolution in years.
coverImageAlt: React Server Components Next.js 2026 — ArivuOn Academy

seo.metaTitle: React Server Components 2026 — Complete Guide with Next.js | ArivuOn Academy
seo.metaDescription: Understand React Server Components in 2026. When to use server vs client components, performance benefits, and real Next.js patterns — ArivuOn Academy.

content.text: React Server Components ship JavaScript to users only when necessary. In a standard React SPA, every component ships to the browser. In a Server Component architecture, components that fetch data, access databases, or perform heavy computation run on the server — and only their rendered HTML reaches the user. The result: dramatically smaller JavaScript bundles and faster load times.
Server Components vs Client Components — The Core Mental Model

In Next.js 14+, every component is a Server Component by default. It runs on the server, can directly access databases and APIs, and sends rendered HTML to the browser. When you need interactivity — event handlers, useState, useEffect — you add 'use client' to the top of the file. That component now ships to the browser and can be interactive. The key insight: most of your UI does not need to be interactive. Navbars, footers, product listings, blog posts — these are perfect for Server Components.

content.title: Server Component — Direct Database Access, Zero Client JS
The Golden Rule of Server vs Client Components
content.text: Push 'use client' as far down the component tree as possible. A page can be a Server Component that fetches data, with one small Client Component button for interactivity. This gives you the best of both: server data access and client interactivity with minimal JavaScript shipped.
Frequently Asked Questions

items.question: Do I need to relearn React for Server Components?
items.answer: Not relearn — extend. Your React knowledge is still valid. Server Components add a new mental model on top: every component is server by default, opt into client with 'use client' when needed. The component model, JSX, props, and composition patterns all work the same way.

items.question: Can I use useState in a Server Component?
items.answer: No — useState, useEffect, and all React hooks that manage client-side state are unavailable in Server Components. This is intentional: Server Components run once on the server, not persistently in the browser. If you need state or interactivity, that specific component must be marked 'use client'.

items.question: Are Server Components only for Next.js?
items.answer: Currently, Next.js App Router is the most mature implementation. Remix and other frameworks are adopting them. React Server Components are a core React feature — they are part of React itself, not Next.js. But as of 2026, Next.js is the primary framework where you would use them in production.

title: Python for AI and Machine Learning in 2026 — The Beginner's Complete Roadmap
excerpt: AI is the fastest-growing engineering discipline on earth. Python is the language of AI. This is the exact roadmap to go from Python basics to building real AI applications.
coverImageAlt: Python for AI and Machine Learning 2026 — ArivuOn Academy

seo.metaTitle: Python for AI and Machine Learning 2026 — Beginner Roadmap | ArivuOn Academy
seo.metaDescription: Learn Python for AI and machine learning in 2026. Complete roadmap from Python basics to building real ML models — by ArivuOn Academy.
keywords: how to learn machine learning python

content.text: AI engineers in India now earn ₹18–50 LPA at mid to senior levels. Globally, ML engineers are among the most sought-after professionals in tech. Python powers all of it — TensorFlow, PyTorch, scikit-learn, Hugging Face, LangChain. The question is not whether to learn Python for AI. The question is where to start and what order to follow.
The Python AI/ML Stack — What You Actually Need
The Exact Roadmap — 6 Months to AI Engineer

steps.title: Python + Math Fundamentals
skills: Linear algebra basics (vectors, matrices, dot products)
steps.why: Every ML algorithm is math. You do not need a degree — but you need enough linear algebra and statistics to understand what your models are doing.

steps.title: Data Analysis with Pandas
steps.why: Real-world ML is 70% data cleaning. Engineers who can wrangle messy data are invaluable. Pandas is the core tool.

steps.title: Classical Machine Learning with scikit-learn
skills: Model evaluation metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1)
steps.why: Gradient boosted trees (XGBoost, LightGBM) still win most tabular data competitions. These classical models are used in production far more than neural networks.

steps.title: Deep Learning with PyTorch
steps.why: PyTorch is the framework of research and production deep learning. Understanding how neural networks work makes you dramatically better at using pre-trained models.

steps.title: LLMs and Generative AI Applications
steps.why: Generative AI is the fastest-growing area in tech. Engineers who can build real LLM-powered products are in extreme demand in 2026.

content.title: The Best Way to Learn ML
content.text: Work on real datasets, not toy examples. Use Kaggle competitions for practice. Pick one project idea that genuinely interests you and build it end-to-end. The engineers who get hired have GitHub projects — not just finished courses.
Frequently Asked Questions

items.question: Do I need a mathematics degree to learn machine learning?
items.answer: No. You need practical math literacy — understanding what a derivative is, what a matrix multiplication does, what variance means. You do not need to derive equations from scratch. Khan Academy's Linear Algebra and Statistics courses cover everything you actually need. Most engineers learn the math on-demand as they encounter it.

items.question: What is the difference between Data Science and Machine Learning Engineering?
items.answer: Data Scientists focus on analysis, insights, and model development — asking 'what does the data tell us?' ML Engineers focus on building reliable, scalable systems that serve ML models in production — asking 'how do we run this model for 10 million users?' Both roles use Python heavily. MLEs typically have stronger software engineering skills. Data Scientists typically have stronger statistical knowledge.

items.question: What AI/ML jobs are available in India in 2026?
items.answer: The hottest roles are: ML Engineer (building production ML systems, ₹20-50 LPA senior), Data Scientist (model development and analysis, ₹15-35 LPA), AI Product Engineer (building LLM-powered features, ₹18-40 LPA), and MLOps Engineer (deploying and monitoring ML in production, ₹18-35 LPA). Companies actively hiring: Flipkart, Paytm, Zepto, Razorpay, PhonePe, all major IT services firms, and hundreds of AI startups.

title: Microservices vs Monolith in 2026 — When to Use Each (The Honest Guide)
excerpt: Every startup wants microservices. Most should not have them. This is the honest, technical breakdown of when microservices make sense — and the expensive mistakes teams make getting it wrong.
coverImageAlt: Microservices vs Monolith Architecture 2026 — ArivuOn Academy

seo.metaTitle: Microservices vs Monolith 2026 — When to Use Each | ArivuOn Academy
seo.metaDescription: Should you use microservices or monolith in 2026? Honest technical comparison, real use cases, and when teams get it wrong — ArivuOn Academy.

content.text: Amazon, Netflix and Uber use microservices. Your 6-month-old startup probably should not. The architecture that powers the world's largest platforms comes with operational complexity that destroys small teams. This guide gives you the framework to decide — without the hype.
What Is a Monolith — And Why It Is Not a Bad Word

A monolith is a single deployable unit containing all application functionality. One codebase, one deployment, one database. Shopify, Stack Overflow, Basecamp, and GitHub all ran as monoliths for years at massive scale. A monolith is not a legacy pattern — it is the right starting point for almost every application. Simple to develop, simple to test, simple to deploy, simple to debug.

content.title: The Rule Most Teams Ignore
content.text: Start as a monolith. Extract a service only when you have a specific, proven pain point that a service would solve — and when your team has the operational maturity to run it. 'We might need to scale this independently someday' is not a valid reason to start with microservices.
Microservices vs Monolith — Honest Comparison

tools: Scaling whole app for one bottleneck

content.text: When to Actually Move to Microservices

steps.title: A specific component needs independent scaling
skills: Checkout service needs 10x more capacity than catalog
Image processing consuming all shared resources
Specific module has 100x more traffic than others
steps.why: If your monolith can scale by adding more instances and that is affordable, do it. Extract a service only when independent scaling of one component becomes economically necessary.

steps.title: Multiple large teams own separate domains
skills: Team A's bugs blocking Team B's deploys
steps.why: Conway's Law: your architecture mirrors your team structure. When team friction from a shared codebase is real and measurable, services can help.

steps.title: Different components need different technology
skills: Each can now use the best tool for the job
steps.why: When one component genuinely needs a different runtime or language that cannot coexist in your monolith, extraction makes technical sense.

content.text: Frequently Asked Questions

items.question: Is it true that microservices are always better for scalability?
items.answer: No. A well-architected monolith running on a load balancer with a read replica can handle significant scale. Stack Overflow serves millions of requests per day from a monolith. The bottleneck for most applications is the database, not the application tier — and microservices do not inherently solve database scaling.

items.question: How do microservices communicate with each other?
items.answer: Two primary patterns: synchronous (HTTP/REST or gRPC — service A calls service B and waits for a response) and asynchronous (message queues like Kafka or RabbitMQ — service A publishes an event, service B consumes it when ready). Asynchronous communication is more resilient but harder to reason about. Most production microservice architectures use both depending on the use case.

items.question: What is the 'modular monolith' approach?
items.answer: A modular monolith is a monolith with strictly enforced module boundaries — each module has its own code, its own database tables, and communicates with other modules through defined interfaces (not direct database queries). It gives you the organizational benefits of microservices (clear ownership, no cross-module data coupling) without the operational overhead. It is the recommended middle ground for most teams in 2026.

title: Git Advanced Workflows in 2026 — Beyond git add, commit, push
excerpt: 90% of developers know basic Git. The 10% who know advanced Git solve problems in minutes that everyone else spends hours on. Rebase, cherry-pick, bisect, reflog — here is how to actually use them.

seo.metaTitle: Git Advanced Workflows 2026 — Rebase, Cherry-Pick, Bisect Explained | ArivuOn Academy
seo.metaDescription: Master advanced Git in 2026. Rebase vs merge, interactive rebase, cherry-pick, git bisect for debugging, and reflog for recovery — ArivuOn Academy.

content.text: Every developer knows git commit and git push. But when a 6-month-old bug appears in production, when a team member commits directly to main, when you need one fix from a branch that is not ready to merge — that is when you need the commands most developers have never used. This guide makes you the developer the team calls when Git goes wrong.
Rebase vs Merge — The Decision That Defines Your Git History

Merge preserves full history — every branch, every merge commit. The history is truthful but can be noisy. Rebase rewrites commits to appear as if they were made on top of the target branch — history is linear and clean but the commit SHAs change. Neither is universally correct. Merge for shared branches where preserving when things happened matters. Rebase for feature branches before merging to keep main history readable.

content.title: Interactive Rebase — Rewrite History Like a Professional
content.code:
# Rebase last 4 commits interactively
git rebase -i HEAD~4

# In the editor that opens:
# pick a1b2c3d First commit
# squash d4e5f6g Fix typo           <- squash into previous commit
# reword h7i8j9k Add feature        <- change commit message
# drop  k0l1m2n Temporary debug     <- remove this commit entirely
# pick  n3o4p5q Final cleanup

# Result: clean, logical history before opening a PR

# Cherry-pick — bring ONE commit from another branch
git cherry-pick a1b2c3d

# Find when a bug was introduced — binary search through commits
git bisect start
git bisect bad                    # current commit has the bug
git bisect good v2.0.0            # this version was fine
# Git checks out middle commit — test it, then tell Git:
git bisect good  # or: git bisect bad
# Repeat until Git identifies the exact commit that introduced the bug

# Recover anything — even after force push or accidental delete
git reflog                        # shows every HEAD movement
git reset --hard HEAD@{3}         # restore to 3 moves ago

content.text: Git Workflows — Which One Should Your Team Use

steps.title: Trunk-Based Development
steps.why: Recommended by Google and Netflix. Small branches mean small merges mean no integration hell. Requires good test coverage and feature flags.

steps.title: GitHub Flow
steps.why: Simple, effective, widely understood. Works well for teams with CI/CD pipelines. Most common workflow in product companies.

steps.title: GitFlow
steps.why: Use for software with discrete version releases (mobile apps, libraries). Too complex for continuous-deployment web applications.

content.title: The One Git Alias Every Developer Should Have
content.text: Add this to your ~/.gitconfig: lg = log --oneline --graph --decorate --all — it gives you a visual tree of your entire branch history in the terminal. Once you use it, you will never go back to plain git log.
Frequently Asked Questions

items.question: When should I use git rebase and when should I use git merge?
items.answer: Rebase your feature branch onto main before merging (keeps main history linear). Never rebase commits that have already been pushed to a shared branch — rewriting shared history breaks everyone's local repos. Merge for integrating long-lived branches or when you want to preserve the exact history of when branches diverged and merged.

items.question: How do I undo a git push --force that broke the team's repo?
items.answer: If someone force-pushed and overwrote commits others have: find the lost commits with git reflog on any machine that had them before the force push. Then git push --force-with-lease with the recovered commits. To prevent this in future: protect your main branch in GitHub/GitLab settings — enable 'Require pull request before merging' and 'Disallow force pushes'.

items.question: What is git stash and when should I use it?
items.answer: git stash temporarily saves your uncommitted changes and reverts your working directory to the last commit — letting you switch branches or pull changes cleanly. Use it when you need to quickly switch context without committing incomplete work. git stash pop restores the changes. Note: stash is for temporary use, not long-term storage. If you stash something important, create a branch instead.

title: PostgreSQL Performance Optimization in 2026 — Stop Your Database from Being the Bottleneck
excerpt: Slow queries are the #1 cause of production performance issues. This guide gives you the exact tools and techniques to find, diagnose, and fix slow PostgreSQL queries in real applications.

seo.metaTitle: PostgreSQL Performance Optimization 2026 — Fix Slow Queries | ArivuOn Academy
seo.metaDescription: Learn PostgreSQL performance optimization in 2026. EXPLAIN ANALYZE, indexes, query optimization, connection pooling and caching strategies — ArivuOn Academy.

content.text: Your application is slow. The team adds more servers. It is still slow. The real problem is almost always the database. A query that takes 2 seconds instead of 2 milliseconds does not just affect one user — it holds a database connection, blocks other queries, and can cascade into a full outage. This guide teaches you to diagnose and fix it.
The Diagnostic Toolkit — Find the Problem Before Fixing It

content.title: Find Your Slowest Queries Right Now
content.code:
-- Enable query statistics (add to postgresql.conf)
-- shared_preload_libraries = 'pg_stat_statements'

-- Find the top 10 slowest queries in your database
SELECT
  round(total_exec_time::numeric, 2) AS total_ms,
  round(mean_exec_time::numeric, 2) AS avg_ms,
  calls,
  round((100 * total_exec_time / sum(total_exec_time) OVER ())::numeric, 2) AS pct_total,
  query
FROM pg_stat_statements
ORDER BY total_exec_time DESC
LIMIT 10;

-- EXPLAIN ANALYZE — see the query execution plan
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT)
SELECT u.name, COUNT(e.id) as enrollments
FROM users u
JOIN enrollments e ON u.id = e.user_id
WHERE u.created_at > '2026-01-01'
GROUP BY u.id, u.name
ORDER BY enrollments DESC
LIMIT 20;
-- Look for: 'Seq Scan' on large tables (needs an index)
-- Look for: high 'actual rows' vs 'estimated rows' (stale stats)
-- Look for: 'Hash Join' on millions of rows (may need index)

content.text: Indexes — The Single Highest-Impact Optimization

steps.title: B-Tree Index — Default, Use for Equality and Range
skills: Multi-column: CREATE INDEX ON orders(user_id, created_at)
steps.why: Turns a sequential scan of 10 million rows into a lookup of 10. This single optimization fixes 80% of slow query problems.

steps.title: Partial Index — Index Only the Rows You Query
skills: CREATE INDEX ON orders(user_id) WHERE status = 'pending'
Use when queries always filter on a specific value
steps.why: If you always query WHERE status = 'pending' and only 1% of rows are pending, a partial index is 100x smaller and faster than indexing all rows.

steps.title: GIN Index — Full Text Search and Arrays
skills: CREATE INDEX ON courses USING GIN(to_tsvector('english', title || ' ' || description))
Also for JSONB and array columns
steps.why: Use when you need real full-text search — 'find all courses mentioning Python'. Far more powerful and faster than LIKE '%python%' which cannot use indexes.

steps.title: Know When NOT to Add an Index
skills: Tables with heavy write load need fewer indexes
Unused indexes waste space and slow writes
steps.why: Indexes are not free. On a table with millions of inserts per day, 10 unnecessary indexes can degrade write performance by 50%. Audit your indexes regularly.

content.title: The Django N+1 Query Problem
content.text: The most common Django performance killer: querying the database once for a list of objects, then querying once more for each object's related data. A page showing 50 courses with their instructors makes 51 queries instead of 1. Fix: use select_related() for ForeignKey and prefetch_related() for ManyToMany. Always check your query count in development with django-debug-toolbar.
Frequently Asked Questions

items.question: How many indexes is too many?
items.answer: There is no fixed number — it depends on your read/write ratio. A read-heavy analytics database can have 20+ indexes on a table. A write-heavy transactional table should have as few as possible — only indexes that appear in frequent, slow queries. Review pg_stat_user_indexes monthly to identify indexes with zero or near-zero usage and drop them.

items.question: When should I use PostgreSQL vs MongoDB?
items.answer: Use PostgreSQL by default — it handles relational, JSON, full-text search, geospatial, and time-series data all in one system. Consider MongoDB only if your data is genuinely document-oriented with highly variable schemas, you need horizontal write scaling at extreme levels, or your team has deep MongoDB expertise. 'Our data is JSON' is not a sufficient reason to use MongoDB over PostgreSQL's JSONB support.

items.question: What is connection pooling and do I need it?
items.answer: PostgreSQL creates a new process for every connection — connecting is expensive. Connection pooling (via PgBouncer or application-level poolers) maintains a pool of database connections that your application reuses. For Django applications, use django-db-geventpool or connect via PgBouncer. If you are seeing 'too many connections' errors or slow response times under load, connection pooling is the fix.

title: API Security in 2026 — The Complete Guide for Backend Developers
excerpt: API security breaches cost companies millions. Most vulnerabilities are not sophisticated attacks — they are basic mistakes that any developer can prevent. This is the checklist every backend developer must follow.
coverImageAlt: API Security Best Practices 2026 — ArivuOn Academy

seo.metaTitle: API Security Best Practices 2026 — Complete Guide for Developers | ArivuOn Academy
seo.metaDescription: Learn API security in 2026. Authentication, authorization, rate limiting, SQL injection prevention, JWT best practices — complete backend security guide by ArivuOn Academy.

content.text: In 2025, API attacks accounted for 83% of all web application breaches according to industry reports. The most common vulnerabilities were not zero-day exploits — they were authentication flaws, missing authorization checks, and exposed sensitive data. Every one of them preventable. This guide is the security checklist every backend developer must internalize.
The OWASP API Security Top 10 — What Actually Gets Exploited

steps.title: Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA)
skills: Check ownership on every object access
Example: /api/orders/456 — verify 456 belongs to the requesting user
steps.why: The #1 API vulnerability for 5 years running. An attacker changes order_id=123 to order_id=124 and reads another user's order. Takes one line of code to prevent, costs millions when exploited.

steps.title: Broken Authentication
skills: JWT: validate signature AND expiry on every request
Refresh token rotation — invalidate old tokens on use
Never store passwords in plain text (use bcrypt/argon2)
steps.why: Weak authentication is the front door being unlocked. Once bypassed, every other security measure is irrelevant.

steps.title: Excessive Data Exposure
skills: Return only fields the client actually needs
Never serialize full ORM objects to API responses
Use explicit serializer fields (whitelist, not blacklist)
Audit every API response for sensitive fields
steps.why: APIs that return full database rows often leak passwords (hashed but still), internal IDs, personal data. Define explicit response schemas.

steps.title: Lack of Rate Limiting
skills: Rate limit all authentication endpoints (max 5 attempts/min)
Return 429 Too Many Requests with Retry-After header
steps.why: Without rate limiting, password brute-force attacks, credential stuffing, and API abuse are trivial. One compromised endpoint can bring down your entire service.

steps.title: Security Misconfiguration
skills: HTTPS everywhere — no HTTP in production
steps.why: Misconfiguration is the most common cause of breaches. A Django app with DEBUG=True in production exposes your entire settings file including database credentials.

content.title: Secure Django API Pattern — BOLA Prevention + Rate Limiting
Never Do This With JWTs
content.text: Never store sensitive data in JWT payload — it is base64 encoded, not encrypted. Anyone can decode it. Never use 'none' algorithm. Always validate the algorithm in the header matches what your server expects. Set short expiry (15-60 minutes) and use refresh tokens for long-lived sessions.
Frequently Asked Questions

items.question: JWT vs Session-based authentication — which should I use?
items.answer: Session-based (server stores session state) for traditional web apps — simpler, more secure by default, easy to invalidate. JWT (stateless — client holds the token) for APIs consumed by mobile apps, SPAs, or third-party clients. JWT's weakness: you cannot truly invalidate a token before expiry without a server-side blocklist, which defeats the 'stateless' benefit. For most Django APIs, JWT with short expiry + refresh token rotation is the right choice.

items.question: How do I protect against SQL injection in Django?
items.answer: The ORM protects you by default — always use it. The vulnerability appears when you write raw SQL with string interpolation: cursor.execute('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ' + user_id) — never do this. If you must write raw SQL, use parameterized queries: cursor.execute('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = %s', [user_id]). The %s placeholder escapes the input safely.

items.question: What security headers should every API have?
items.answer: Critical: Strict-Transport-Security (forces HTTPS), X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff (prevents MIME sniffing), X-Frame-Options: DENY (prevents clickjacking). For APIs: remove X-Powered-By and Server headers (do not advertise your stack). For SPAs: Content-Security-Policy. In Django, use django-security or manually add these in your middleware. Check your headers at securityheaders.com.
